title: FAANG Written Communication Culture
description: How FAANG engineers communicate through writing — weekly updates, design docs, RFCs, performance reviews, strategy memos, and async collaboration. Covers FAANG's document-driven culture vs SAP's meeting-driven culture, the Google Docs collaboration model, and how to become an effective technical writer as an SAP engineer.

description.value:
At FAANG, every significant communication starts as a document. Design docs, strategy memos, project proposals, performance reviews, even meeting agendas are written documents that people read before discussing them. Meetings exist to debate a document that everyone has already read, not to start from a blank whiteboard.

This is the single biggest cultural difference from SAP. In SAP, the default communication mode is the meeting: schedule a call, discuss verbally, assign action items, and then someone writes minutes afterward. The meeting drives the document. At FAANG, the document drives the meeting — or often replaces it entirely.

Google Docs is the primary collaboration tool at FAANG. Every design doc is a living Google Doc with inline comments, suggestion mode edits, and threaded discussions that accumulate over days or weeks. By the time a meeting happens, participants have already read the doc, left comments, and formed opinions. The meeting is for resolving remaining disagreements, not for catching up.

The implication for SAP engineers: if you join FAANG, your first habit to unlearn is scheduling a meeting to "talk through" an idea. Instead, write a one-page doc, share it with stakeholders, add comments, and see if the meeting is even needed. Most of the time, it isn't.

decisionTree.question: You have a complex technical proposal. Should you write a doc first or schedule a meeting?

branches.condition: You are at FAANG or a FAANG-style company
branches.action: Write a one-page Google Doc first. Share it with 3-5 stakeholders, set a 48-hour comment deadline, then decide if a meeting is needed based on comment volume. Fewer than 10 comments? Skip the meeting and decide async.
branches.tradeoff: Builds async culture but can feel slow when urgency is high.

branches.condition: You are at SAP or a meeting-driven company
branches.action: Still write the doc first, but schedule a 30-minute meeting to walk through it. Use the meeting to drive decisions while the doc captures them. This bridges both cultures.
branches.tradeoff: More preparation work but produces better documentation and clearer decisions.

branches.condition: You have a deadline in under 24 hours
branches.action: Schedule the meeting but write a minimal agenda doc (5 bullet points). During the meeting, assign someone to capture decisions in the doc. The doc is the source of truth, not the chat transcript.
branches.tradeoff: Pragmatic triage; sacrifices async depth for speed.

description.value:
FAANG companies prioritize asynchronous communication — writing before meeting, documenting before deciding, sharing before discussing. The core rule is simple: write a doc before scheduling a meeting. If the doc is clear enough, the meeting may not be needed at all.

SAP engineers default to synchronous communication. An engineer has a question? Schedule a call. A proposal needs feedback? Book a workshop. A decision needs sign-off? Set up a steering committee meeting. The default is to bring people into a room (physical or virtual) and talk it through. This works, but it has a hidden cost: calendar latency. A decision that could be made in 24 hours via a shared document takes 5 business days because that's the earliest everyone's calendars align.

FAANG's async culture scales better for distributed teams across time zones. A Google Doc shared across SV, London, and Bangalore gets comments round-the-clock. A meeting across those time zones means someone always attends at 2 AM.

The FAANG rule of thumb: write first, meet later, meet only if necessary. A good heuristic is the "two-meeting rule": if you can't resolve the decision in two async rounds of document comments, then schedule a focused 30-minute discussion. Otherwise, close the decision in the doc.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "My manager at FAANG prefers verbal stand-ups and doesn't read my weekly update documents. Should I keep writing them or adapt to their verbal preference?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "I'll stop writing updates and just talk to them in person." (abandons paper trail, loses visibility across the org, and creates knowledge silos — your impact becomes invisible to skip-level leaders)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "I'll write the document as always, then send a two-sentence Slack summary highlighting the key decision or blocker. For the verbal manager, I make the doc skimmable: bold the single most important line, put the ask in the first paragraph, and include a tl;dr. If they want to discuss, we have a 5-minute chat — but the doc remains the source of truth. For my own career, I also ensure skip-level leaders and stakeholders have access to the doc, so my impact is visible beyond my direct manager."
adversarialResponse.pattern: adapt-without-abandoning — meet the manager where they are while preserving the written record. The doc is not just for your manager; it's for your future self, your skip-level, your performance review packet, and cross-functional stakeholders who discover your work months later.

sections.title: The Weekly Update — Your Most-Read Document

description.value:
The weekly update is the single most-read document you will ever write at FAANG. Directors, VPs, and skip-level managers read weekly updates to track progress across teams. Your weekly update is your visibility vehicle — it's how leaders outside your immediate team know what you're doing and why it matters.

The FAANG weekly update format is brutally concise: 3-4 bullets on what you did, 1-2 bullets on what you learned, blockers (if any), and next week's plan. Keep it under 200 words. No fluff, no context that the reader should already know, no self-promotion — just facts.

What SAP engineers get wrong: they write too much. They include background context, explain why the work is important, and describe technical deep-dives. FAANG leaders scan 50+ weekly updates per week. If yours is over 200 words, they stop reading after the first paragraph. Write for the executive skim test: can a VP understand your impact in 10 seconds?

Format tips: Use bold for the single key takeaway. Put the blocker or ask in the first line (not buried at the bottom). If you need a decision, end with a clear call to action: "Decision needed: approve the CDC migration plan by Friday." Attach links to relevant docs, not the docs themselves. Your update is a pointer to deeper content, not the content itself.

sections.codeBlock:
Weekly Update Template (150-200 words)

Done This Week
- Shipped [feature/change] resulting in [metric impact]
- Reviewed [N] design docs for [project]
- Unblocked [team/person] on [specific issue]

Key Learnings
- [Technical insight or pattern discovered this week]
- [Tool/process improvement identified]

Blockers
- [⚠] Awaiting [review/approval/input] from [person/team] on [ticket #]

Next Week
- [Top priority deliverable]
- [Secondary goal]
- [People/career item — mentoring, interview, doc review]

---
Decision needed: [One-line ask with deadline] → Reply with '+1'/'lgtm' or comment by [date]

sections.title: Design Docs and RFCs at FAANG

description.value:
FAANG design docs and RFCs (Request for Comments) follow a rigorous writing process that differs significantly from SAP's design documentation culture. In SAP, design documents are often written after implementation for compliance or handover purposes. At FAANG, design docs are written before implementation, reviewed by peers, and iterated through multiple rounds of feedback before a single line of code is written.

Getting Started: Every FAANG design doc starts with a one-paragraph problem statement. If you can't articulate the problem in 3 sentences, you don't understand it well enough to design a solution. The doc then covers: context and motivation, goals and non-goals, proposed design, alternatives considered, system architecture (with diagrams), APIs and data model, migration plan, and open questions. The template is standardized within each company — Google has its own RFC format, Amazon has its PR/FAQ, Meta has its design doc template.

Getting Reviews: Share the doc early — when it's 30% complete, not when it's "done." FAANG engineers review in Google Docs with inline comments and suggestion mode. The key metric is comment velocity: if no one comments within 24 hours, the doc is either perfect (unlikely) or nobody cares (dangerous). Proactively tag specific reviewers on sections you want feedback on. Use the "@" mention liberally.

Iterating Based on Feedback: Every comment deserves a response. Accept, address with a counter-proposal, or defer to a follow-up. The worst thing you can do is resolve a comment without acknowledging it. FAANG culture rewards being responsive to feedback — stubbornness on design is a negative signal at Staff+ levels because it indicates inability to collaborate at scale.

Closing Out: When the design is approved, the doc becomes a living reference. Update it as implementation reveals new constraints. FAANG teams often add an "implementation notes" section at the bottom that captures deviations from the original plan. This turns the design doc into a historical record that future engineers can learn from.

sections.codeBlock:
FAANG Design Doc / RFC Template

Title: [Project Name] — Design Doc v0.1
Author: [Name]  Date: [Date]  Status: Draft / Review / Approved

1. Problem Statement
[3 sentences: what is broken, who is affected, why now]

2. Goals & Non-goals
Goals:
- [Concrete outcome, measurable]
Non-goals:
- [What we explicitly are NOT solving]

3. Proposed Design
[System diagram, key components, data flow with cardinalities]
[API contracts (REST/gRPC), data models, storage schema]

4. Alternatives Considered
- Alternative A: [approach] → [why rejected: tradeoff]
- Alternative B: [approach] → [why rejected: tradeoff]

5. Migration Plan
Phase 1 (Week 1-2): [minimal viable change]
Phase 2 (Week 3-4): [full rollout]
Rollback strategy: [how to undo]

6. Open Questions
- [ ] [Question] → Owner: [Name]  Deadline: [Date]
- [ ] [Question] → Owner: [Name]  Deadline: [Date]

---
Reviewers: @alice @bob @carol — Please comment by [Date]

sections.title: Performance Review Writing — Telling Your Story

description.value:
FAANG performance reviews are written documents, not verbal discussions. At Google (Perf), Meta (PSR), Amazon (Forté), and Apple, you write a self-evaluation that presents your accomplishments, impact, and growth over the review period. Your manager and calibration committee evaluate you based on what you write — not on hallway conversations or meeting contributions.

This is a profound shift for SAP engineers. In SAP, performance is often determined by project delivery, utilization rate, and manager observation. At FAANG, your ability to document your impact in writing directly determines your rating, promotion, and compensation. Two engineers with identical accomplishments can get different ratings because one writes a compelling narrative and the other writes a dry list of tasks.

The FAANG perf review formula: For each accomplishment, state: (1) the problem or opportunity, (2) what you did (specific actions, not general responsibilities), (3) the measurable impact (users, revenue, latency, reliability, team throughput), and (4) the leadership signal (did you mentor, influence without authority, set technical direction?).

What SAP engineers get wrong: They write responsibility-based descriptions ("I led the implementation of the new pricing module") instead of impact-based descriptions ("Identified that the pricing module's query pattern caused 3-second P99 latency. Redesigned the data access layer with a materialized view cache, reducing P99 to 40ms — a 75x improvement that unblocked real-time pricing across 3 regions.") The difference is specificity and quantification.

FAANG promotion packets require even more rigor. At Staff+ levels, your packet must demonstrate org-wide impact, influence beyond your team, and technical leadership that changed how your organization builds systems. Every bullet needs a scope multiplier: "This pattern was adopted by 5 teams" or "This decision saved 200 engineering hours per quarter across the org."

sections.codeBlock:
Performance Review Self-Eval Template

Accomplishment 1: [Title — e.g., "Pipeline Latency Optimization"]
[Star: S/T/A/R + Impact + Leadership]

Story: Our data ingestion pipeline processed 50M events/day but P99 latency degraded to 12s after a 3x volume increase, violating our 5s SLA and causing downstream dashboard delays for 15 internal teams.

Action: I profiled the pipeline and identified that the bottleneck was a single-threaded enrichment step that performed sequential API calls. I redesigned it as a partitioned, async workflow using gRPC streaming with backpressure, implemented circuit breakers per partition, and added adaptive concurrency control.

Result: P99 latency dropped from 12s to 1.8s (6.7x improvement). Pipeline now handles 200M events/day without degradation. The pattern was documented and adopted by 3 other teams in the org.

Leadership: Mentored 2 junior engineers on async pipeline patterns. Influenced the data platform team to adopt gRPC streaming as a standard pattern. Presented at the all-hands tech talk on real-time data processing.

---
Accomplishment 2: [Title]
[Repeat STAR format for next achievement]

Growth Areas (Self-Reflection):
- [Honest assessment of what you're working to improve]
- [Concrete steps you're taking with measurable progress]

description.value:
The deepest insight in FAANG's document culture is that writing is not just communication — it is thinking. The act of writing forces clarity. If you can't write it clearly, you haven't thought it through. This principle is why FAANG requires written design docs before implementation, written strategy memos before roadmapping, and written performance reviews before calibration.

Writing exposes gaps in your thinking that conversation hides. In a verbal discussion, you can hand-wave, change topics, or rely on charisma to fill the gaps. In a written document, every gap is visible. Ambiguities in your architecture become obvious when you try to describe the data flow in prose. Missing dependencies surface when you list them. Weak arguments collapse when you write them down because the reader has time to analyze — they can't be distracted by your confidence or enthusiasm.

Jeff Bezos banned PowerPoint at Amazon and required written narratives for exactly this reason. The famous "six-pager" is not a document format; it's a thinking discipline. Amazon meetings start with 30 minutes of silent reading because the writing itself is the output. The discussion that follows is sharper because everyone has already thought deeply about the content.

For SAP engineers: The writing-to-think principle is your superpower. SAP's complexity demands clear thinking — integration across 20 systems, data consistency across heterogeneous landscapes, compliance constraints that cascade through layers. If you write these down, you not only communicate your design — you discover its flaws before they become production incidents. Start a personal design journal. Write out your architecture decisions even when no one asks for them. The clarity you gain will make you a better engineer, not just a better communicator.

Practical exercise: Pick a complex system you know well — your SAP S/4HANA landscape, a BTP integration flow, a custom ABAP application. Write a one-page explanation of how it works, assuming the reader is a competent engineer who knows nothing about SAP. If you can't explain the architecture in one page, you don't understand it deeply enough. The gaps in your writing are gaps in your understanding. Fill them.

This exercise is also your best interview preparation. FAANG system design interviews test exactly this skill: can you take a vague problem ("design YouTube") and produce a clear, structured written explanation of your architecture? Engineers who practice writing-to-think outperform those who only practice drawing diagrams, because the writing forces the structure that the diagram illustrates.

details: Staff+ Insight: FAANG's written culture is the single biggest communication adjustment for SAP engineers. In SAP, meetings are where decisions happen; documents record the decisions. In FAANG, documents are where decisions happen; meetings socialize the decisions. If you can write clearly, you can influence at FAANG without being the loudest person in the room. This is actually an advantage for introverted SAP engineers who are clear thinkers — your writing will be read by VPs and Directors who would never sit through a 2-hour design workshop. Invest in your writing skills. It's the highest-leverage communication investment you can make.
